title: Startup Finance Literacy Challenge

introduction.subtitle: Knowledge quiz. 8-10 min.
introduction.description:
Startup Finance Literacy Challenge is a learning challenge for the terminology, assumptions, and typical decision traps behind Unit Economics, Cash & Runway, Cap Table & Dilution, Financial Statements. It is designed to teach through plausible wrong answers as much as through the correct ones.

Choose the answer you would use in a real conversation. After completion, the answer review explains the concept and points to the area worth studying next.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: Which formula correctly calculates gross margin percentage?

options.text: A. Revenue minus all operating expenses, divided by revenue.
B. Revenue minus cost of goods sold, divided by revenue.
C. Net income divided by cash collected.
D. Revenue divided by customer acquisition cost.

conceptHelp.title: Unit Economics: Which formula correctly calculates gross margin percentage
conceptHelp.summary: Unit Economics means the economics of one customer, transaction, or unit of activity, including margin, acquisition cost, payback, and contribution. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "Which formula correctly calculates gross margin percentage?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Revenue minus cost of goods sold, divided by revenue."

questions.text: A customer costs EUR 600 to acquire and generates EUR 150 of monthly gross profit. What is the simple CAC payback period?

options.text: A. 2 months.
B. 4 months.
C. 6 months.
D. The answer requires annual recurring revenue only.

conceptHelp.title: Unit Economics: A customer costs EUR 600 to acquire and generates EUR 150 of monthly gross profit. What is the simple CAC payback period
conceptHelp.summary: Unit Economics means the economics of one customer, transaction, or unit of activity, including margin, acquisition cost, payback, and contribution. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "A customer costs EUR 600 to acquire and generates EUR 150 of monthly gross profit. What is the simple CAC payback period?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.

questions.text: A company has EUR 600,000 in cash and a stable monthly net burn of EUR 50,000. Ignoring future changes, what is its runway?

options.text: A. 6 months.
B. 10 months.
C. 12 months.
D. 50 months.

conceptHelp.title: Cash & Runway: A company has EUR 600,000 in cash and a stable monthly net burn of EUR 50,000. Ignoring future changes, what is its runway
conceptHelp.summary: Cash & Runway means how cash balance, burn, collections, payment timing, and growth investments determine time before funding pressure. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "A company has EUR 600,000 in cash and a stable monthly net burn of EUR 50,000. Ignoring future changes, what is its runway?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.

questions.text: How can a fast-growing company report increasing revenue while running out of cash?
questions.explanation: working capital and upfront growth costs can consume cash before collection.

options.text: A. Revenue growth always increases cash, so this cannot happen.
B. Customers may pay later while inventory, hiring or acquisition costs are paid earlier.
C. Only accounting fraud can create this situation.
D. It occurs only when gross margin is above 100%.

conceptHelp.title: Cash & Runway: How can a fast-growing company report increasing revenue while running out of cash
conceptHelp.summary: Cash & Runway means how cash balance, burn, collections, payment timing, and growth investments determine time before funding pressure. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "How can a fast-growing company report increasing revenue while running out of cash?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Customers may pay later while inventory, hiring or acquisition costs are paid earlier."

questions.text: An investor invests EUR 2 million at an EUR 8 million pre-money valuation. What is the investor post-money ownership before other adjustments?

conceptHelp.title: Cap Table & Dilution: An investor invests EUR 2 million at an EUR 8 million pre-money valuation. What is the investor post-money ownership before other adjustments
conceptHelp.summary: Cap Table & Dilution means how ownership changes when shares, options, convertible instruments, and financing terms are added. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "An investor invests EUR 2 million at an EUR 8 million pre-money valuation. What is the investor post-money ownership before other adjustments?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.

questions.text: A new option pool is created immediately before a financing and included in the pre-money valuation. Who generally bears most of that dilution?
questions.explanation: a pre-money option pool top-up primarily dilutes existing holders.

options.text: A. The new investor only.
B. Existing shareholders before the financing.
C. Future employees only.
D. Nobody, because option pools do not dilute ownership.

conceptHelp.title: Cap Table & Dilution: A new option pool is created immediately before a financing and included in the pre-money valuation. Who generally bears most of that dilution
conceptHelp.summary: Cap Table & Dilution means how ownership changes when shares, options, convertible instruments, and financing terms are added. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "A new option pool is created immediately before a financing and included in the pre-money valuation. Who generally bears most of that dilution?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Existing shareholders before the financing."

questions.text: Which statement shows assets, liabilities, and shareholders equity at a point in time?
questions.explanation: the balance sheet presents financial position at a point in time.

options.text: A. Income statement.
B. Cash flow statement.
C. Balance sheet.
D. Sales pipeline report.

conceptHelp.title: Financial Statements: Which statement shows assets, liabilities, and shareholders equity at a point in time
conceptHelp.summary: Financial Statements means how income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement each explain a different part of financial reality. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "Which statement shows assets, liabilities, and shareholders equity at a point in time?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.

questions.text: Which statement best explains why a company can have positive EBITDA but declining cash?
questions.explanation: the cash flow statement reconciles working capital, capex, and financing flows.

options.text: A. The cash flow statement, because it includes working capital, capital expenditure and financing flows.
B. The cap table, because it records all cash movements.
C. The product roadmap, because it recognises depreciation.
D. Positive EBITDA guarantees positive cash flow.

conceptHelp.title: Financial Statements: Which statement best explains why a company can have positive EBITDA but declining cash
conceptHelp.summary: Financial Statements means how income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement each explain a different part of financial reality. This question teaches the concept through a concrete prompt: "Which statement best explains why a company can have positive EBITDA but declining cash?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The plausible wrong answers usually contain familiar words but miss the mechanism, calculation, trade-off, or evidence standard that makes the best answer stronger.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by the rule they use. Prefer the answer that would still be defensible in a real founder, investor, finance, or product conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "The cash flow statement, because it includes working capital, capital expenditure and financing flows."

questions.text: A SaaS product has EUR 20,000 MRR and EUR 8,000 gross profit after direct hosting and support costs. What is gross margin?
questions.explanation: Gross margin is gross profit divided by revenue: EUR 8,000 / EUR 20,000 = 40%.

options.text: D. It cannot be calculated from MRR and gross profit.

conceptHelp.title: Unit Economics: A SaaS product has EUR 20,000 MRR and EUR 8,000 gross profit after direct hosting and support costs. What is gross margin
conceptHelp.summary: Unit Economics is tested here through a concrete operating question: "A SaaS product has EUR 20,000 MRR and EUR 8,000 gross profit after direct hosting and support costs. What is gross margin?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.

questions.text: Which action improves runway without reducing monthly operating burn?
questions.explanation: Collecting cash earlier improves cash balance and runway even if the cost base is unchanged.

options.text: A. Collecting annual prepayments from customers who would otherwise pay monthly.
B. Hiring ahead of plan because bookings are strong.
C. Moving all revenue recognition into the current month.
D. Changing EBITDA presentation without changing cash collections.

conceptHelp.title: Cash & Runway: Which action improves runway without reducing monthly operating burn
conceptHelp.summary: Cash & Runway is tested here through a concrete operating question: "Which action improves runway without reducing monthly operating burn?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Collecting annual prepayments from customers who would otherwise pay monthly."

questions.text: A SAFE has a valuation cap below the priced round valuation. What is the usual effect of the cap?
questions.explanation: The valuation cap gives the SAFE holder a lower conversion price than the priced round price, resulting in more shares.

options.text: A. It usually gives the SAFE holder a lower conversion price and more shares than the priced-round price.
B. It prevents any dilution for founders.
C. It automatically gives the SAFE holder board control.
D. It only matters if the company is already profitable.

conceptHelp.title: Cap Table & Dilution: A SAFE has a valuation cap below the priced round valuation. What is the usual effect of the cap
conceptHelp.summary: Cap Table & Dilution is tested here through a concrete operating question: "A SAFE has a valuation cap below the priced round valuation. What is the usual effect of the cap?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "It usually gives the SAFE holder a lower conversion price and more shares than the priced-round price."

questions.text: Which question cannot be answered from the income statement alone?
questions.explanation: Runway requires cash balance and cash burn, so the balance sheet and cash flow view are also needed.

options.text: A. What is gross profit for the period?
B. What was operating expense for the period?
C. How many months of runway remain?
D. What revenue was recognised in the period?

conceptHelp.title: Financial Statements: Which question cannot be answered from the income statement alone
conceptHelp.summary: Financial Statements is tested here through a concrete operating question: "Which question cannot be answered from the income statement alone?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "How many months of runway remain?"

questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. The quiz is meant to teach the vocabulary before it tests recall.
